title: Reclaiming air-dry clay
subtitle: Reconstituting dried scraps and failed pieces into workable clay
excerpt: Air-dry clay is relatively expensive compared to kiln-fired stoneware, and a bag of trimmings and failed pieces can represent a significant waste. Reclaiming turns dry or leather-hard scraps into fresh, workable clay. The process takes two to three days but requires almost no active work.
sourceNotes: Clay reclaim is described in studio pottery practice. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Air-dry clay scraps, trimmings, and leather-hard failures can be reconstituted into fresh, workable clay through the
reclaim

process. The method is the same as kiln-fired stoneware reclaim, scaled to the domestic context. Bone-dry pieces can be reclaimed; glazed or painted pieces cannot (the surface treatment is not reversible). Do not mix clay bodies in the same reclaim bucket.

attrs.body: Fired clay, pieces sealed with PVA or acrylic, and pieces with underglaze painting are not reclaimable. Polymer clay is a plastic and cannot be reclaimed at all. Only unfired, untreated air-dry or paper clay can be reclaimed.
attrs.title: What cannot be reclaimed

content.text: What you need

items.name: Dry or leather-hard air-dry clay scraps
Large bucket or old mixing bowl

content.text: The reclaim method
Step 1: Break scraps into small pieces and saturate with water

Break or crumble the dry clay scraps into pieces no larger than 3 cm across. Place them in the bucket and pour water over until all pieces are submerged. Leave to soak for at least four hours, longer for thick pieces. The clay will absorb water and begin to break down. Stir once after two hours to help the process.
Step 2: Allow to become slop
After soaking, the clay should have broken down into a thick
slop

: a paste that pours slowly and holds its shape briefly before spreading. If lumps remain, mash them with the back of a large spoon or simply leave for another few hours. Pour off any standing water that has separated above the clay slop.
Step 3: Spread on the plaster bat

Pour or scoop the clay slop onto a dry plaster bat to a depth of about 2 cm. The plaster absorbs water from the clay rapidly; the clay stiffens from the bottom up. Leave undisturbed for two to four hours. The clay is ready to turn when the upper surface looks matte and dry rather than shiny.
Step 4: Turn and even the drying

Flip the slab of clay off the plaster bat onto a canvas board. The base that was against the plaster is now on top. Leave for a further two to four hours until the clay throughout the slab feels the same firmness. If the edges dry faster than the centre, fold them in and pat flat.
Step 5: Wedge to homogenise

Once the clay is firm and pliable throughout, it needs wedging to remove any air pockets and even out the moisture content. Knead the clay as you would bread dough, pressing the heels of both hands forward then folding the far edge back. Ten minutes of wedging is enough for a 500 g block. The clay is ready when a wire cutter slice through the block shows no air holes.
Without a plaster bat

Without a plaster bat, spread the clay slop in a thin layer on a canvas board or wooden board outdoors on a warm day, or inside on a radiator shelf. Turn the slab every few hours to avoid uneven drying. The process takes twice as long as the plaster bat method. Canvas works better than wood because it releases cleanly.
Common problems

attrs.intro: Issues that arise during clay reclaim.

items.fix: Leave for another four hours or overnight. Do not force lumps: they will break down with time.
items.cause: Some pieces were too thick or the soaking time was too short.
items.symptom: Clay is still full of lumps after soaking

items.fix: Move the clay to a dry canvas board and allow it to continue drying there. Dry out the plaster bat in a warm location before reuse.
items.cause: Too much water was added to the bucket, or the plaster bat has become saturated itself.
items.symptom: Reclaimed clay is still too soft after two days on the plaster bat

items.fix: Mist the surface of the clay slab with water and cover with plastic for two hours before wedging.
items.cause: Dried too quickly on the bat, producing uneven moisture distribution.

attrs.heading: Common problems
